author: @tiekauntan3264
text: Great video as always. I learned alot from your videos and I am always impressed at how you make fancy exclusive stuff like graphene and nanotubes with simple kitchen chemistry. I got somewhat excited when you said you got a resistance of less than 200 ohms across the glass when earlier you mentioned you were coating graphene oxide. Found out later in the comments you reduced it to graphene after coating, so oh well. Another thing is that piece of glass look relatively heavy and I was worried the tape will fail and the glass will fly off at an angle cutting you. Please be careful! (though this vid is 4 years old)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
Dean Hunt
lol - no worries mate - you are asking good questions but they have been asked often - so often that i did quite a bit on this to stop me having to write the same reply a few hundred times - it's worth getting up to speed as this is a fascinating subject where serious work and new discoveries can be done in the kitchen - enjoy

author: @kablive
text: Graphene is a one atom thick material that is a better conductor than copper or silver, and is the strongest substance known.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Do you really want to get in a long discussion on this? Just give it a go for your own peace of mind. I have no interest in helping you prove how much more clever you are than me. You probably are but discussing it just doesn't interest me.

author: @guybus
text: Graphite is an electrical conductor and anything at a small enough thickness can be effectively transparent. Graphene however is a single monolayer of carbon atoms (therefore has a predetermined finite thickness).

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: but that doesn't mean you shouldn't try it

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: It's a question of area not conductivity so probably not

author: @guybus
text: Whats the evidence you have for this creating graphene and not graphite?

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: I'm afraid this is where experimentation comes in. Look at Erik Goeken's vids and try drop casting different concentrations of your GO, making up a sample cell and seeing how long it will light an led with a standard charge - say 1.5 volts for 10 seconds. You could also try different electrolytes and different separators once you have found a good concentration. I can't be of more help - some questions are best answered by doing.

author: @TheIdeanator
text: Any recommendations for optimal thicknesses of the graphene plates and dielectrics?

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Oddly enough having an ultra thin coating of graphene for use in a supercapacitor is a bad idea. What you want is a relatively thick coating. This is because of the way supercapacitors work

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Probably not. There is world of difference between what is theoretically possible and what is actually achievable. For example as a supercapacitor material it supposed to have an achievable capacitance of 500F per gram  but it is actually getting around 90F per gram. Pretty rubbish really but that's the way it is.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Electrostatics, van der walls - a whole host of reasons that govern wetting behaviour. I don't know how easily it would peel off but it would be interesting to know as the glass could serve as an intermediate for coating plastic film. The ticker coating is more conductive or rather less resistive. I can't measure conductivity so I measure resistivity and use it as a guide for conductivity. No probs on the multimeter cheers
